basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: An-Nās — “Mankind” — is the one hundred and fourteenth and final sūra of the Quran. Together with the preceding sūra, al-Falaq, it forms the Muʿawwidhatān, “the two protectresses”: the two sūras of refuge. Both open with the same command — Say: I seek refuge… But where al-Falaq protects from outward harms, an-Nās guards the most intimate place: the heart. It asks for refuge from the whisperer, the one who breathes doubt within man.

versets.trad: Say: “I seek refuge with the Lord of mankind,
versets.commentaire: Three divine Names open the sūra, like an ascent. The first is Rabb, the Lord. The commentators read these three verses as a single indication: to find true refuge from the tempter, one must first acknowledge the complete sovereignty of God — that He alone is worshipped, He alone may be called upon for help — unlike those who take other lords apart from Him. To seek refuge is thus to recognise oneself as wholly dependent: the very opposite of the illusion of self-sufficiency.

mots.gram: preposition bi- + noun in the genitive

versets.commentaire: The second Name: Malik, the King. It is by forgetting that God is the King of mankind that the human being arrogates to himself a sovereignty that is not his own. The Quran gives this its figure in Pharaoh, who dares to say: “Is not the kingdom of Egypt mine?” To know oneself under a King is to give up the claim to be one's own master.

The third Name: Ilāh, the God — the sole One towards whom worship goes. The ascent is complete: He brings into being (Rabb), He governs (Malik), He alone is worshipped (Ilāh). Pharaoh carries forgetfulness to its end: “I know of no god for you other than myself.” And so man is here called to seek refuge from that illusion of self-sufficiency — the root of every sin — by confessing his total dependence: O my Lord, O my King, O my God.

versets.trad: from the harm of the furtive whisperer,
versets.commentaire: al-waswās al-khannās: the stealthy whisperer. Waswasa is the murmur — a suggestion uttered low and endlessly repeated. If the tempter is called khannās, “the one who withdraws,” it is because he slips from the heart the moment man remembers God, and returns the moment man forgets Him. The first sin of Adam and Eve was precisely to lend an ear to this murmur. Elsewhere the Quran names him the Deluder: he seduces, makes one forget, makes evil seem fair, desires to lead astray — a manifest enemy.

versets.trad: who whispers doubt into the breasts of mankind —
versets.commentaire: He whispers into the breasts of mankind — ṣudūr, the inmost chamber of the heart. Here it is the tempter who murmurs; yet elsewhere the Quran describes the soul itself as whispering and prompting to evil. Not the soul as such, but the soul that commands to evil (al-nafs al-ammāra) — of which a famous ḥadīth says: “Satan flows through man as the blood flows.” From one view, the tempter suggests and the passionate soul follows its caprice rather than God's command; from another, only the soul that commands to evil bears this inclination. And the Quran warns: to whoever turns away from the remembrance of the Compassionate, He assigns a satan as companion.

versets.trad: whether of jinn or of mankind.”
versets.commentaire: The tempter is of two orders: the jinn — the unseen — and mankind. Evil counsel does not come only from a hidden world; it reaches us also through other human beings, for there are tempters among jinn as among men. The verse may also be read: that which whispers doubt into the hearts of jinn no less than of men — the tradition recalls that there was a “people” among the jinn. And the whole Quran ends upon this word, an-nās, “mankind,” repeated like a refrain throughout the sūra: the Book closes upon man, the one it has, from beginning to end, sought to awaken.
